message.content: qual a diferenca do token salvo com '//' no inicio no meu ~/.npmrc?

content.thinking:
O usuário está perguntando sobre a diferença de um token salvo com '//' no início no arquivo ~/.npmrc. Isso é uma pergunta sobre configuração do npm.

Vou responder em inglês conforme as instruções do CLAUDE.md.

content.text:
In `~/.npmrc`, lines starting with `//` are **registry-scoped authentication tokens**, not comments.

## Format

```ini
//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=npm_xxxxxxxxxxxx
//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxx
```

## Key differences

| Syntax | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `//registry.example.com/:_authToken=TOKEN` | Auth token scoped to a specific registry URL |
| `_authToken=TOKEN` | Global auth token (applies to default registry) |
| `; comment` or `# comment` | Actual comments (ignored by npm) |

## Why `//` prefix?

The `//` matches the protocol-relative URL pattern. npm strips `https:` from URLs internally, so:

- `https://registry.npmjs.org/` becomes `//registry.npmjs.org/`
- This allows the token to match requests to that specific registry

## Common patterns

```ini
# Global default registry token
_authToken=npm_xxxx

# Scoped to npmjs
//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=npm_xxxx

# GitHub Packages
//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=ghp_xxxx

# Private registry
//nexus.company.com/repository/npm/:_authToken=xxxxx

# Scoped packages pointing to different registry
@mycompany:registry=https://nexus.company.com/repository/npm/
```

The `//` syntax is essential when you need different tokens for different registries (e.g., public npm + private company registry).

message.content: é por isso que no npmrc local do projeto costuma funcionar mesmo sem o '//'?

content.thinking:
O usuário está perguntando se é por isso que no .npmrc local do projeto costuma funcionar mesmo sem o '//' - ou seja, usando apenas `_authToken=TOKEN` diretamente.

Vou explicar que sim, isso acontece porque no .npmrc local do projeto geralmente já existe uma configuração de `registry=` que define o registry padrão, então o token global (sem //) se aplica a esse registry.

content.text:
Exactly. In the local project `.npmrc`, it usually works without `//` because:

## The token inherits the registry context

```ini
# Project .npmrc
registry=https://nexus.company.com/repository/npm/
_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}
```

Here, `_authToken` automatically applies to the registry defined above it. npm interprets this as "use this token for the configured registry".

## vs. Global `~/.npmrc`

```ini
# Global ~/.npmrc needs to be explicit
//nexus.company.com/repository/npm/:_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}
//registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=${PUBLIC_NPM_TOKEN}
```

In global config, there's no single registry context—you may access multiple registries across different projects—so you must scope each token explicitly with `//`.

## Summary

| Location | Common pattern | Why |
|----------|----------------|-----|
| Project `.npmrc` | `registry=X` + `_authToken=Y` | Token inherits the registry defined in same file |
| Global `~/.npmrc` | `//registry/:_authToken=Y` | No single context; must scope explicitly |

## Caveat

If your project uses multiple registries (e.g., scoped packages from different sources), you'll still need the `//` syntax even in the local `.npmrc`:

```ini
registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/
@company:registry=https://nexus.company.com/npm/
//nexus.company.com/npm/:_authToken=${PRIVATE_TOKEN}
```
